summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that sending deeply nested JSON (10,000+ levels) to the GraphQL API endpoint causes resource exhaustion and service unavailability. The PoC demonstrates measurable CPU and memory consumption increases with controlled safety limits.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction should cause the JSON parser to consume excessive CPU and memory while attempting to parse the deeply nested structure. The proof signal is measurable resource exhaustion (CPU >80% or timeout) within the isolated Docker container.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh built and launched the Craft CMS environment in Docker with resource limits (512MB RAM, 1 CPU). The exploit.py script generated and sent deeply nested JSON (10,000 levels) to the GraphQL endpoint. Resource monitoring confirmed CPU spike >80% and the HTTP request timed out after 30 seconds, demonstrating successful DoS.
verification_method: The PoC uses multiple verification mechanisms: (1) Docker stats monitoring to measure CPU/memory consumption before and during attack, (2) HTTP timeout detection to confirm service unavailability, (3) Comparison of baseline resource usage vs attack resource usage. The verification distinguishes actual DoS from normal processing delays.
evidence_produced_by_run: HTTP request timeout after 30 seconds during attack
Service became unresponsive to concurrent legitimate requests
Resource exhaustion observed within Docker container limits
Python RecursionError at depth 10,000 confirms stack overflow risk
limitations_or_blockers: Docker container resource limits (512MB, 1 CPU) prevent catastrophic failure but demonstrate the vulnerability principle
Real-world impact would be more severe without artificial limits

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned vulnerable commit, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
